title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, June 2
tldr: Trump signed the AI executive order he'd canceled on May 25 — reframed as a “voluntary” 30-day pre-release review for “covered frontier models,” with no licensing but a new Treasury/NSA/CISA security clearinghouse.

Microsoft Build: in-house foundation models. MAI-Thinking-1 (reasoning) and MAI-Code-1-Flash (5B, default-eligible in GitHub Copilot) — trained without OpenAI data. Redmond is hedging its OpenAI dependence.
OpenAI shipped GPT-5.5 + Codex GA on AWS Bedrock — OpenAI is now on every major cloud (Azure, AWS, Oracle) right before its IPO path.

blocks.sub: Trump's AI EO: “voluntary” review with coercive gravity
blocks.p: On Jun 2 the White House issued Promoting Advanced AI Innovation and Security — the order pulled on May 25, redesigned. Treasury + NSA + DHS get a classified process to label models with “advanced cyber capabilities” as covered frontier models; labs that opt in submit them for review 30 days before public release and choose “trusted partners” for early access. Explicitly no licensing or permitting, plus an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse via Treasury/NSA/CISA.
blocks.why: Last week's “US federal picture is unsettled” call flipped fast: this is a soft licensing regime in voluntary clothing. Once the big three opt in (early access wins government contracts), smaller labs face an implicit “opt in or look reckless.” It's also the public-sector mirror of Anthropic's Glasswing — government wants the same dual-use security access labs are selling.
blocks.doIt: If you ship anything plausibly a “covered frontier model” (any agent strong at code/security), start the audit artifacts now: model card, eval suite, red-team report, training-data provenance — the same package the EU AI Act needs by Aug 2026. Build once, file twice.

blocks.sub: Microsoft Build: the hyperscaler that no longer needs OpenAI's models
blocks.p: At Build (Jun 2) Microsoft shipped its first in-house models — MAI-Thinking-1 (reasoning) and MAI-Code-1-Flash (5B coding model, rolling out as a default-eligible option in GitHub Copilot), both trained on commercially-licensed data, no OpenAI distillation.
blocks.why: The April Microsoft–OpenAI exclusivity drop wasn't a one-off — it was the starting gun. Copilot is now a model-agnostic product, and “built on clean data” is becoming an enterprise selling point as IP suits pile up.
blocks.doIt: If you build on Copilot, instrument per-task model attribution in your logs now — default routing will start silently sending cheap requests to MAI-Code-1-Flash, and you want to catch quality regressions before users do.

blocks.sub: OpenAI GPT-5.5 + Codex GA on AWS Bedrock
blocks.p: Also Jun 2: GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4, and Codex went GA on Amazon Bedrock (spend applies against AWS commits). Codex reportedly passed 5M weekly users.
blocks.why: Enterprises with AWS commits lose their last procurement excuse to skip OpenAI. Shipping on AWS the same day Microsoft ships in-house models is both sides publicly de-coupling — right before OpenAI's IPO.

blocks.sub: Learn “covered frontier model” — the new regulatory chess piece
blocks.p: The EO defines scope by capability (advanced cyber ability, classified benchmark), not parameter count or FLOPs. That means a small fine-tuned open-weight model could qualify if it gets too good at finding vulnerabilities — you can't know at training time. Build your eval pipeline so you can measure cyber-offensive capability (CTF/vuln-discovery task suites) the way you measure benchmarks. And treat “voluntary” as a future market floor: voluntary frameworks (HIPAA Safe Harbor, SOC 2) become customer-demanded badges within ~24 months. Plan to be compliant by mid-2027.

sources: White House (Promoting Advanced AI Innovation and Security EO, Jun 2) · CNBC / NBC News / A&O Shearman (EO analysis) · Microsoft AI / GitHub Changelog (MAI-Thinking-1, MAI-Code-1-Flash, Build 2026) · GuruFocus (Trump EO + MAI-Code-1-Flash) · AWS News Blog / OpenAI (GPT-5.5 + Codex GA on Bedrock). Figures directional.
